segments.s103-g41: اندر آورد is a compound, word‑order typical of older/poetic Persian: 'به بند کمرش اندر آورد چنگ' literally 'into the band of his waist he brought (his) claw/hand.' Here به + بند کمرش marks motion into the belt (بند کمر = belt/girdle) and چنگ (claw/hand) is the thing brought; کمرش shows the enclitic possessive -ش 'his'.
segments.s103-g42: جدا کردش is composed of جدا (apart) + کرد (did/made) + enclitic -ش (him); together 'he separated him.' از پشت زین is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the saddle' (پشت = behind; زین = saddle) and پلنگ tags that saddle (the panther) as possessive/epithet; word order places the verb کردش before the locative phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s103-g43: همی before a past tense verb (خواست) is a literary progressive marker: 'was just/were about to.' بردنش is بردن + enclitic -ش (take him). پیش قباد means 'before Qobad / in Qobad's presence' with پیش functioning as a locative/prepositional particle.
segments.s103-g44: This clause is tersely compressed: دهد (infinitive or present/optative 'to give' or 'he gives') followed by روز جنگ (the day of battle) and نخستینش داد (literally 'the first he gave'). In context this means he had dealt or given the opponent the first day/measure of battle — داد is the past 'gave.' The doubled give-forms are a poetic repetition; روز جنگ = 'day of battle' and نخستینش = 'its first/first of it.'
segments.s103-g45: ز هنگ is a poetic genitive/ablative 'from/because of the time/commotion of' (ز = from, هنگ = commotion/time). سپهدار و چنگ سوار lists two actors: 'the commander and the cheng‑savar (literally “claw‑rider”, here an epithet).' In older Persian compounds like چنگ سوار can be an epithet for a mounted fighter; و simply connects the two.
segments.s103-g46: نیامد here is negative past 'did not come' but idiomatically means 'did not hold/appear/stay' — common in poetry. دوال کمر (دوال = twofold/pair + کمر = waist/girdle) is the subject and پایدار ('steady/lasting') is a predicate adjective; the verb's negative sense applies to 'the paired girdle did not remain steady.'
segments.s103-g47: گسست is a short past 'it broke/separated.' The coordination و connects what follows: به خاک اندر آمد سرش is literal 'to earth into came his head' (اندر آمد = came into/ fell), so word order is inverted by poetic style; سرش uses enclitic -ش 'his'.
segments.s103-g48: سواران گرفتند is straightforward past 'the horsemen seized.' گرد اندرش is a locative phrase 'around/about him' (گرد = around; اندر = in/among; ش = his), so the clause means the horsemen closed in and seized those surrounding him.
segments.s103-g73: برفتند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of رفتن, meaning “they left/they departed.” Here the verb precedes its subject (ترکان), a common classical/Persian poetic word order (V–S). ز پیش مغان is a prepositional phrase: ز (poetic form of از) = “from,” پیش = “before/in the presence of,” and مغان = “the Magi,” so the whole phrase means “from before the Magi / having left the Magi’s presence.”
segments.s103-g74: کشیدند is the past 3rd-person plural of کشیدن, here used intransitively/causatively to mean “they moved/advanced (something).” It takes لشگر (“army”) as its object, and سوی + place indicates the direction: سوی دامغان = “toward Damghan.” The clause therefore states that they moved/led the army toward Damghan.
segments.s103-g75: وزانجا is a fused particle meaning “and from there.” به جیحون is a prepositional phrase “to/toward the Jeyhun (Amu Darya).” نهادند is the past 3rd-person plural of نهادن “to place/set”; here it is part of the idiom روی نهادن/نهادن روی = “to turn one’s face toward.” روی (literally “face”) is the direct object of نهادند, so the clause means “they set/turned their face toward the Jeyhun.”
segments.s103-g76: This is a verbless descriptive phrase giving the manner: خلیده دل is an adjectival phrase meaning “with withdrawn/retiring (خلیده) heart (دل)” — i.e., downcast or withdrawn in spirit. و (and) links the next prepositional phrase: با غم و گفت‌وگوی = “with sorrow (غم) and with conversation/talk (گفت‌وگو),” indicating they were talking in a mood of grief. Overall this modifies the preceding clause (their manner of turning toward Jeyhun).
segments.s103-g77: شکسته and گسسته are past-participle/adjectival forms meaning “broken”/“torn.” Each modifies the following noun: شکسته سلیح = “weapons broken,” and گسسته کمر = “belt/waist severed” (کمر here = girdle/belt). The conjunction و links the two parallel descriptive clauses, describing the ruined condition of their arms and attire.
segments.s103-g78: This is a series of negative nouns introduced by the repeated negator نه … و نه … meaning “neither … nor …” and lacking an explicit verb (a common poetic verbless construction listing absences). بوق = trumpet/horn, کوس = war-drum, پای = “foot,” and سر = “head”; the line lists the things that are missing (signals, drum, and even foot or head).
segments.s103-g65: بران is a prepositional/locative particle here meaning 'upon/on them' and introduces what is placed on/against those people. The noun phrase ترگ زرین و زرین سپر lists two items in parallel (ترگ 'a rag/shred/garment' + زرین 'golden' and زرین سپر 'golden shield'), with و simply coordinating them; order is noun + adjective (زرین postposed before سپر due to poetry).
segments.s103-g66: This clause uses the copula شد (past 'became') with غمی as the predicate nominal and سر as its subject (word order is inverted for emphasis: predicate before subject). از introduces the source/cause ('from'), and the reduplicated adjective-like sequence چاک چاک (literally 'split, split') intensifies the gash inflicted by تبر (axe). Overall: 'the head became sorrow/grief because of the axe's gaping gash.'
segments.s103-g67: گفتی is a 2nd-person past tense verb 'you said' introducing a reported clause; که is the subordinating particle 'that' leading into the reported content. ابری is an indefinite subject 'a cloud' and برآمد is the simple past 'arose/issued forth'; ز کنج gives the source 'from the corner.'
segments.s103-g68: ز marks the source ('from'), شنگرف names the shining/ornamental thing from which the action comes, نیرنگ is the grammatical subject here meaning a 'stratagem/feint' or artifice, and زد is the simple past 'struck' (used transitively); بر is the preposition 'upon/on' and ترنج is the object 'ornamental medallion' that the stratagem strikes. Word order: source + subject + verb + prepositional object.
segments.s103-g69: ز introduces origin/agency 'from' and is followed by گرد سواران, where گرد functions as a collective 'troop/company' (here used with سواران 'horsemen') — an idiomatic classical usage rather than the literal 'around' listed in the sense options. در آن پهن دشت is a locative phrase 'in that wide/plain plain' modifying where the troop is located.
segments.s103-g70: زمین شش شد is a terse, poetic clause where زمین 'the earth' is subject and شش شد 'became six' is the predicate (numeric complement after شد); و links the parallel clause آسمان گشت هشت where گشت (a synonym of 'became/turned') is used instead of شد. The phrasing is hyperbolic/poetic: numbers act as predicate complements.
segments.s103-g71: The numeral compound هزار و صد و شصت ('one thousand one hundred sixty') precedes the noun گرد, here a collective 'troop/company' (classical usage), and دلیر is a postpositive adjective 'brave' describing that collective. Word order: number + collective noun + adjective.
segments.s103-g72: به یک زخم uses به in an instrumental/agentive sense 'by/with' (indicating the cause or instrument) followed by یک زخم 'a single wound.' شد کشته is a passive-like/periphrastic construction 'became killed' meaning 'were slain.' چون introduces a simile 'like,' and نره شیر ('male lion') completes the simile describing how they were killed.
segments.s103-g57: چنان تا is a result/consequence connector: چنان (an adverb 'thus/so') + تا (conjunction 'that'), introducing the clause that follows. بر شاه ترکان is a prepositional phrase (بر = 'to/toward') with شاه ترکان = 'the king of the Turks' (شاه modified by ترکان). رسید is the simple past 'reached/arrived' (3rd singular); the subject is omitted (contextual 'news/word').
segments.s103-g58: درفش سپهدار is a genitive/possessive grouping 'the banner/standard of the spahbed (commander)'. شد is the past of شدن used as a copula 'became', and ناپدید is a predicative adjective 'vanished/was invisible' — so the clause literally says the spahbed's banner 'became vanished'.
segments.s103-g59: گرفتش is a past verb (گرفت) with the enclitic object pronoun -ش ('him/it') = 'he seized him/it'. کمربند is the direct object 'the belt'. و simply links the next verb. بفگند is an archaic/poetic form of 'بفکند' (to cast/throw) — here past action 'he hurled/threw'. خوار is adjectival/adverbial 'lowly/despised' describing how he was cast.
segments.s103-g60: خروشی is a noun 'a cry/roar'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. ترکان is 'the Turks' as the source of the sound. برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'. بزار here is an archaic/onomatopoeic/adverbial word indicating lamentation or a plaintive cry; it is not one of the standard modern senses and functions as an adverbial exclamation modifying the rising cry.
segments.s103-g61: ز جای اندر آمد is a small clause 'he came forth from his place' with the prepositional phrase ز جای اندر (from his place/within his post) + آمد (past 'came'). چو introduces a simile: چو آتش قباد = 'like fire (was) Qobad' (the name follows the simile in poetry). چو here means 'like' and the whole phrase modifies آمد.
segments.s103-g62: بجنبید is a past poetic form meaning 'was set in motion / stirred'. لشگر is the subject 'the army/host'. چو دریا ز باد is a simile 'like the sea from the wind' (چو = 'like', ز = 'by/from' indicating cause: 'by the wind'); together it says the army moved as a sea is moved by the wind.
segments.s103-g63: برآمد = 'arose/issued forth' (past). خروشیدن is a verbal noun 'shouting/roaring' functioning as one of the things that arose. دار و کوب is a paired nominal phrase: کوب ('beating/striking') is clear; دار here is an older/poetic lexeme whose usual modern senses ('house', 'gallows', 'tree') do not capture its martial/onomatopoeic use in the line (effectively part of the compound meaning 'shouting and beating' / 'clamor and blows').
segments.s103-g64: درخشیدن is a verbal noun 'the flashing' or 'gleaming'. خنجر = 'dagger' is the instrument whose flashing is described. و links the next phrase. زخم چوب = 'wound/injury of/by stick' where زخم is 'wound' and چوب here means 'stick/club' (i.e., blows from wooden clubs). The whole segment lists sounds/sights of the battle.
segments.s103-g17: همه روی آهن گرفته به زر is a compact descriptive clause: همه روی ('the whole surface') + آهن گرفته (literally 'taken/turned to iron', a past-participial use of گرفتن meaning 'covered/made of iron') + به زر ('with gold' / 'overlaid in gold'). Word order is typical Persian: determiner/quantifier - noun - participial phrase - prepositional complement. The participle گرفته qualifies روی and links to the following prepositional phrase به زر as an instrument/material.
segments.s103-g18: نشانی سیه بسته بر خود بر is another compact participial/nominal phrase: نشانی سیه ('a black device/emblem') + بسته (past participle 'fastened/tied') + بر خود بر ('upon itself' — بر ... بر is a poetic repetition/emphasis meaning 'on'). The participle بسته describes the relation of the mark to the object; بر خود indicates the mark is fixed on that very thing.
segments.s103-g19: ازو خویشتن را نگه‌دار سخت is an instruction: ازو ('from him' / 'against him') + خویشتن را ('yourself' with object marker را) + نگه‌دار ('keep/guard' — here used as an imperative or hortative) + سخت ('firmly / strictly'). The object-marker را attaches to خویشتن; نگه‌دار is a verb-form used as 'keep/guard' and سخت modifies the manner.
segments.s103-g20: که مردی دلیرست و پیروز بخت is the clause giving the reason: که ('for/because') + مردی ('a man') + دلیرست (adjectival-predicative 'is brave') + و ('and') + پیروز بخت ('fortunate/victorious in fortune' — a compound of پیروز 'victorious' and بخت 'fortune'). Here that-clause explains why one should be on guard.
segments.s103-g21: بدو گفت رستم که‌ای پهلوان is the reporting clause with direct address: بدو ('to him') + گفت (the verb 'said', here used as past narrative) + رستم (subject) — word order is inverted for poetic effect — followed by a quotative/attention particle که and ای پهلوان ('O champion' vocative). The particle که often introduces the following quoted speech.
segments.s103-g22: تو از من مدار ایچ رنجه روان is the quoted admonition: تو ('you') + از من ('because of me' / 'on my account') + مدار (here negative/admonitory sense 'do not cause/bring' or 'do not take upon' — an archaic/poetic verbal form) + ایچ رنجه ('any trouble/affliction at all', ایچ = nothing/none + رنجه = suffering) + روان (literally 'going' or 'in peace/at ease' — final word indicates 'depart' or 'be free'). Together it tells the addressee not to trouble himself over the speaker and to go without distress.
segments.s103-g23: جهان آفریننده یار منست is a short predicative clause: جهان آفریننده ('the world's Creator' — a compound noun) + یار ('friend/ally') + منست (an archaic/copula-like form meaning 'is mine' or 'is to me' / 'is', here functioning as 'is'). It states that the Creator (or God) is the speaker's ally. Note منست is an older poetic form rather than modern است.
segments.s103-g24: دل و تیغ و بازو حصار منست lists the speaker's defenses: دل ('heart') + و تیغ ('and sword') + و بازو ('and arm/strength') + حصار ('fortress/protection') + منست (again the copular/possessive poetic form 'is / are mine'). The nouns are coordinated and then summed up by حصار to mean these things together constitute his defence.
segments.s103-g25: برانگیخت is a past, transitive verb here functioning as 'lifted/raised' (3rd person). آن رخش رویینه سم is the object phrase: آن 'that', رخش 'steed', رویینه an adjectival epithet 'armored/iron-clad' (postpositive), and سم 'hoof' — so the verb takes the whole noun phrase 'that iron-shod steed’s hoof' as its object. The word order Verb + object is poetic but normal in Ferdowsi.
segments.s103-g26: برآمد is an intransitive past 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن is a deverbal noun/infinitive 'the roaring' or 'a roar' (used as the thing that arose). گاو دم is a compact descriptive phrase meaning 'of the ox / ox‑like'—literally 'cow' + 'breath/tip' but here functioning idiomatically to qualify the sound as ox‑like (i.e. a deep bellow). The sequence is idiomatic: 'a roar arose (like) an ox's'.
segments.s103-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. افراسیابش combines the proper name افراسیاب with the enclitic -ش (him), so the clause reads 'when Afrasiab saw him' or 'when he saw Afrasiab' depending on context; here it functions as 'when Afrasiab saw him'. به هامون is a locative 'in/on the plain' (به + place). بدید is the past 'saw'.
segments.s103-g28: شگفتید is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'were amazed' (past). ازان introduces the content/cause clause 'at/that' (literally 'from that'), linking the astonishment to what follows. کودک 'child/infant' is the noun being spoken about. نارسید is the negative past 'did not arrive' (نـ- prefix + رسید past form), so together: 'they were amazed that that child had not come.'
segments.s103-g29: ز ترکان بپرسید is 'he asked the Turks' (ز = 'of/from' used poetically to mark source/agent, ترکان = 'the Turks', بپرسید = past 'asked'). کین here functions as an interrogative/demonstrative 'what/this' (archaic/poetic; effectively 'which/what is this'), followed by اژدها 'dragon' as the noun being questioned. (Note: کین is archaic here and not the common noun 'vengeance'.)
segments.s103-g30: بدین گونه is a frozen phrase 'in this manner / thus' (ب- as a prefix + دین/این fused form in poetry + گونه 'way, manner'). از بند گشته رها is a verbal phrase: از بند 'from the fetter/bond', گشته (perfect participle/become) + رها 'free/unbound' — together 'has become free from its bonds' or 'has thus broken loose'. Word order places the manner phrase up front (بدین گونه) followed by the result.
segments.s103-g31: کدامست is the interrogative 'Which is it?/Who is he?'. کین را uses the demonstrative/archaic interrogative کین ('this/that/which') plus the object marker را — here functioning as 'this one' or 'that fellow' as the object. ندانم is the 1st‑person present 'I do not know'. به نام is the idiom 'by name' ('به' here = 'by' or 'in the name of'). The whole line reads: 'Which is he? I do not know him by name.'
segments.s103-g32: یکی گفت means 'someone said' (یکی = 'someone/a certain one', گفت = 'said' — note the lemma list given treats گفت as 'conversation' but here it functions as the verb 'said'). کاین is the demonstrative 'this/that'. پور is the noun 'son/of the son' (used as 'the son of ...'), دستان is an epithet literally 'hands' used here as 'strong‑handed' (an epithet of Sam), and سام is the personal name 'Sam'. The clause reports: 'Someone said: "This is Sam's son, the strong‑handed."'
segments.s103-g33: نبینی is a negative second-person present used rhetorically — “do you not see…”; که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. با گرز سام is a prepositional phrase (با + noun + proper name) meaning “with Sam’s mace” (preposition + object + possessor), and آمدست is an archaic/poetic third-person perfect/past form meaning “has come.” The clause ends with the verb, as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s103-g34: جوانست is the fused form of جوان است (“is young”), a simple predicative clause. و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” جویای نام is an adjectival/participial phrase meaning “seeking (جویای) a name,” where نام here means “reputation/fame”; آمدست again is the poetic perfect/past “has come,” so the whole phrase reads “and (he) has come seeking fame.”
segments.s103-g35: به پیش سپاه is a locative/prepositional phrase meaning “before/in front of the army” (به + پیش + noun). آمد is the past tense verb “came,” and افراسیاب as subject appears after the verb (a common poetic order: locative phrase + verb + subject) meaning “Afrasiab came forward before the army.”
segments.s103-g36: چو introduces a simile (“like/as”). کشتی is the head noun “ship,” and the relative clause که موجش برآرد ز آب modifies it: موجش (“its wave”) is the subject of برآرد, a poetic present/regular verb meaning “raises/lifts,” and ز آب is the poetic prepositional phrase “from the water.” The literal order is “like a ship that its wave lifts from the water.”
segments.s103-g37: چو means “when” (introducing a temporal clause). رستم ورا دید is “Rostam saw him” (چو + subject + object + verb). بفشارد is a poetic/narrative form with the b- prefix meaning a past action here (“he gripped/pressed”), and ران is the object “thigh.” The b- prefix is common in epic diction to mark the narrative action.
segments.s103-g38: بگردن is an adverbial/prepositional phrase meaning “around/on the neck.” برآورد here is a poetic past form of آورد used to mean “he placed/lifted (it) onto,” and گرز گران is the direct object “the heavy mace.” Together the phrase reads “(he) put/lifted the heavy mace onto (his) neck.”
segments.s103-g39: چو again means “when.” تنگ اندر آورد is an idiomatic/poetic causative phrase meaning “drew (him) in close/tightly” (تنگ = tight/close, اندر آورد = brought in). با او and زمین are a coordinated prepositional/nominal element “with him (to) the ground” that links to the verb in the next segment (i.e. ‘he threw him to the ground’). In other words, this segment sets up the action (‘when he drew him tight…’) and the accompanying phrase ‘with him to the ground.’
segments.s103-g40: فرو is an adverbial element meaning “down(ward),” and کرد is the past-tense verb “did/made” — together فرو کرد = “drove down/forced down.” گرز گران را is the definite object marker (را) attached to “the heavy mace.” به زین indicates direction/placement “into/onto the saddle.” The whole clause means “he drove the heavy mace down into/onto the saddle.”
segments.s103-g1: چو at the start is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رستم is the subject. بدید is the simple past 'saw'. آنک introduces a subordinate content-clause ('that/which') referring forward to the action/person; قارن is the person named. چه کرد is an embedded question-clause 'what (he) did' functioning as the object of بدید. Word order is archaic but typical: conjunction + subject + verb + subordinate 'that' + noun + interrogative + verb.
segments.s103-g2: چه‌گونه is an interrogative 'what sort/how'. بود is the past/copular verb 'was' describing Qaren. ساز here is used poetically as 'maker/author' (i.e., the one who brings about something) rather than a musical instrument. ننگ و نبرد is a coordinated noun phrase 'disgrace and battle' that together form the predicate 'maker of disgrace and battle'. The whole clause asks about Qaren's character/role.
segments.s103-g3: به پیش پدر شد is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'he went up to / approached his father' (به + پیش = 'towards the presence of'); شد is the past-marker/verb indicating the action was completed. بپرسید is the main reporting verb 'asked' (past 3rd sing.). از وی marks the person from whom he inquired ('of him' / 'asked him'). Word order: directional phrase + verb of motion + reporting verb + source.
segments.s103-g4: که here introduces the content of the question/command ('that' / 'which'). با normally means 'with' and governs the following pronoun من 'me' ('speak with me' / 'be with me'). جهان پهلوانا is an archaic/poetic plural or vocative 'world-champions' (پهلوان with plural/vocative ending -ا). بگوی is a jussive/subjunctive/imperative-form of گفتن used to report the requested speech/action ('would speak' / 'speak'). Together this clause gives the content of what he asked.
segments.s103-g5: که opens this relative/content clause 'that'. افراسیاب is the proper name. آن is a demonstrative 'that' (emphatic), and بد اندیش is a compound adjective 'bad‑thinking' = 'ill‑intentioned / ill‑minded'. مرد is the noun 'man'. The phrase labels Afrasiab as 'that ill‑thinking man'; word order is demonstrative + adjective + noun.
segments.s103-g6: کجا جای گیرد is an interrogative 'where will (he) take/place/stand'—جای 'place' + گیرد (third-person form of گرفتن) used in the sense 'to take/occupy (a place)'. به‌روز here means 'on the day (of...)' and modifies نبرد 'battle'. The clause therefore asks where he will be positioned when battle comes.
segments.s103-g7: چه پوشد is an interrogative 'what will (he) wear' — چه 'what/how' + پوشد (present/jussive 3sg of پوشیدن) expressing the expected/epic action. کجا برافرازد درفش is another interrogative 'where will he raise aloft the banner' (کجا 'where' + برافرازد present/jussive 3sg of برافراشتن 'to raise' + درفش 'banner'). These are parallel questions about appearance and deployment in battle.
segments.s103-g8: که here introduces the explanatory clause 'for/that'. پیداست is a fused predicate 'is evident/visible' (پیدا + ست 'is'); تابان is an adjective 'shining/bright', and درفش بنفش is the noun phrase 'purple banner' (درفش 'banner' + بنفش 'purple'). The clause states plainly that the purple banner is clearly visible, explaining why the earlier question about where it will be raised is relevant.
segments.s103-g49: سپهبد is the subject (‘the spahbed’). چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning ‘when’. از + چنگِ رستم is a prepositional phrase ‘out of Rostam’s grip’ (چنگ used in the sense of a hold/clutch). بجست is an archaic/synoptic past form (third person singular) meaning ‘leapt’ or ‘bounded away’, so the whole segment is a temporal subordinate clause: ‘when the spahbed leapt out of Rostam’s grip’.
segments.s103-g50: This clause contains the verb-like form بخائید (here functioning archaically as the main action), the subject رستم and the phrase پشت دست ‘back of the hand’. همی is the habitual/immediate aspectual marker (here giving a sense of immediacy or continued action). Word order places the verb-form before the subject; taken together it yields ‘Rostam (immediately) struck (or smote) on the back of the hand’. Note: بخائید is an archaic/poetic form and does not match the modern senses listed exactly.
segments.s103-g51: چرا here is the interrogative/exclamatory ‘why’ introducing reported speech. گفت is the reporting verb ‘said’ (used with the following direct content); نگرفتمش is first-person past negative ‘I did not take/catch him/it’ with the clitic ش referring back to the object; زیرکش is the object ‘undershirt/vest’. Together this reports speech: ‘He said, “Why — I did not take his undershirt.”’
segments.s103-g52: همی marks a continuing or immediate action (‘had just’ / ‘was about’). بر کمر is the prepositional phrase ‘about the waist’. ساختم is first-person past 'I made/formed', so the quoted speaker is using the first person. بند خوش is a noun phrase ‘a good/pleasant band’ (بند = band/belt). The clause therefore reads in reported speech as ‘I had made a good band around the waist.’
segments.s103-g53: چو introduces another temporal clause ‘when’. آوای زنگ is a genitive construction ‘the sound/voice of the bell’ (آواِِ زنگ). آمد is past ‘came’. از پشت پیل is a locative phrase ‘from behind the elephant’. The whole segment means ‘when the sound of the bell came from behind the elephant’.
segments.s103-g54: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive used as the predicate ‘to roar / the roaring (began)’. کوس is the subject ‘the war-drum(s)’. بر چند میل is a prepositional phrase meaning roughly ‘over/for several miles’ (بر = over/on, چند = several). میل here denotes distance (several miles) in context; that particular sense of میل is not among the provided sense options. Overall: ‘the drums roared for several miles.’
segments.s103-g55: یکی here means ‘someone’ or ‘a certain person’. مژده is ‘good news’ or ‘announcement’. بردند is third-person plural past ‘they carried/brought’ (so an implicit plural agent ‘they’ brought the news). نزدیک شاه is the goal phrase ‘to/near the king’. The clause: ‘Someone brought news to the king.’
segments.s103-g56: که introduces a content clause ‘that…’. رستم is the subject. بدرید is past of دریدن ‘tore’ (here used metaphorically). قلب سپاه is a genitive phrase ‘the heart (core) of the army’ — i.e. a decisive blow to the army. Together: ‘that Rostam had torn the heart/core of the army.’
segments.s103-g9: This is the clause's subject and temporal adverb plus its direct object. من (man) is the 1st‑person subject 'I'; امروز (emruz) is a temporal adverb 'today' placed after the subject (common in poetry). بند کمرگاه اوی is a genitive noun phrase 'the strap/band of his waist' where اوی is an archaic possessive 'his'.
segments.s103-g10: This string contains three verb phrases in sequence describing the speaker's intended actions. بگیرم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive ('I seize' / 'I will seize') used here as a resolved intention; کشانش is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'drag him' (object suffix -ش = 'him'); بیارم is the 1st‑person form of 'bring' (here 'I will bring'); بروی is another verb form ('you go' / 'you depart') in the dictionary list but in the line functions idiomatically to close the series of actions (rendered here 'bring him to you'). The verbs are coordinated without conjunctions — a common poetic compression.
segments.s103-g11: بدو (be‑u) is the archaic prepositional/clitic form 'to him' (i.e. 'said to him'); گفت is the past tense 'said' (the provided lexical senses list is for the noun 'conversation', so the exact verbal sense is not in the list); زال‌ای is a vocative 'O Zāl' or here identifies the speaker (Zāl) followed by the address; پسر is a vocative 'son'; گوش‌دار is an epithet meaning 'heedful/attentive' used in direct address. Note the order: indirect object + verb + vocative + noun + epithet — typical of epic speech.
segments.s103-g12: یک امروز is an emphatic temporal phrase 'this very day' (یک as an intensifier with 'today'). با خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'with yourself' (خویشتن = reflexive 'self'), and هوش‌دار is an exhortative adjective 'be alert' or 'be on your guard' — here functioning like an imperative addressed to the addressee. The preposition با + reflexive emphasizes self‑vigilance.
segments.s103-g13: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'for' or 'that'. آن ترک is 'that Turk' (demonstrative + noun). در جنگ is the locative phrase 'in battle' (در + جنگ). نر اژدهاست is a nominal predicate with poetic copula -است attached to اژدها 'dragon'; نر ('male') qualifies the metaphor: 'a male dragon' = very fierce. Word order: [subordinator] + [NP] + [locative] + [predicate].
segments.s103-g14: در آهنگ و در کینه uses repeated در + noun for emphasis: 'in assault/attack (آهنگ here = intentions/assaults) and in hatred (کینه)'. ابر بلاست is a metaphorical predicate 'he is a cloud of calamity/disaster' where ابر (cloud) + بلا (calamity) form a compound image; the copula -است is again fused to بلا. The sequence paints martial ferocity and vengefulness poetically.
segments.s103-g15: درفشش (darfash‑ash) is the subject 'his standard/banner' (the possessive -ش attached). سیاهست is the predicate 'is black' (سیاه + copula -است). و links the coordinate clause; خفتان سیاه repeats the descriptive pattern: khuftān (an item(s) of his equipment — the exact antiquated term is here used nominally) + سیاه (black). The copula is omitted in the second clause by poetic ellipsis but understood.
segments.s103-g16: This is a compressed material‑of construction using the poetic preposition ز 'from/of': ز آهنش ساعد literally 'from his iron (is) the forearm' = 'his forearms are of iron' (آهنش = 'his iron', possessive -ش). The second half mirrors the first: ز آهن کلاه 'from iron (is) the helmet' = 'his helmet is of iron.' The copula is omitted; ز indicates the material making the item.

sentences.s103-s21: He thrust his hand into his belt and tore him away from behind the panther's saddle.
sentences.s103-s22: He was about to carry him before Qobad; he had already (given/inflicted) the first day of battle.
sentences.s103-s23: Because of the turmoil of the commander and the clawed rider, the pair of girdles about the waist would not hold.
sentences.s103-s24: It broke, and his head fell to the ground; the horsemen seized those around him.
sentences.s103-s37: The Turks departed from before the Magi and moved their army toward Damghan.
sentences.s103-s38: And from there they set their faces toward the Jeyhun (Amu Darya), with withdrawn hearts and in sorrowful talk.
sentences.s103-s39: Their weapons were broken and their belts rent; there was neither horn nor drum, neither foot nor head.
sentences.s103-s33: Upon them (were) a golden rag and a golden shield; the head became grief from the gaping gash of the axe.
sentences.s103-s34: You said that a cloud rose from the corner; from the bright sheen a stratagem struck upon the medallion.
sentences.s103-s35: From the troop of horsemen in that broad plain, the earth became six and the sky became eight.
sentences.s103-s36: One thousand one hundred sixty brave troops were, by a single wound, slain — like male lions.
sentences.s103-s29: Thus, when it reached the Turkish king, the spahbed's banner vanished.
sentences.s103-s30: He seized his belt and flung him down in contempt; a cry of lamentation rose from the Turks.
sentences.s103-s31: From his place he sprang forth like fire — Qobad; the army was stirred like the sea by the wind.
sentences.s103-s32: There rose shouting and beating; the flashing of daggers and the wounds dealt by clubs.
sentences.s103-s9: Its entire surface was covered with iron and overlaid with gold, and it bore a black emblem upon itself.
sentences.s103-s10: Guard yourself well against him, for he is a brave man and fortunate in his lot.
sentences.s103-s11: Rostam said to him, 'O hero, do not trouble yourself on my account; go without any hardship.'
sentences.s103-s12: The Creator of the world is my ally; heart, sword, and arm are my fortress.
sentences.s103-s13: That iron-shod steed lifted its hoof; an ox-like bellow arose.
sentences.s103-s14: When Afrasiab saw him on the plain, they were astonished that the child had not arrived.
sentences.s103-s15: He asked the Turks, 'What is this dragon that has thus broken loose from its bonds?'
sentences.s103-s16: Which is he? I do not know him by name. Someone said, 'This is the son of Sam, the strong‑handed.'
sentences.s103-s17: Do you not see that he has come with Sam's mace? He is young, and he has come seeking a name (seeking fame).
sentences.s103-s18: Afrasiab came forward before the army, like a ship whose wave lifts it from the water.
sentences.s103-s19: When Rostam saw him, he gripped his thigh and lifted the heavy mace onto his neck.
sentences.s103-s20: When he drew him in tight, he threw him to the ground, and he drove the heavy mace down into the saddle.
sentences.s103-s1: When Rostam saw what Qaren had done and what sort of author of shame and battle he was.
sentences.s103-s2: He went before his father and asked him which of the world's champions would speak with him.
sentences.s103-s3: Where will that ill-intentioned Afrasiab take his place on the day of battle?
sentences.s103-s4: What will he wear, where will he raise his banner? For the purple banner is clearly shining.
sentences.s103-s25: When the spahbed leapt out of Rostam's grasp, Rostam (immediately) struck on the back of the hand.
sentences.s103-s26: He said, ‘Why — I did not take his undershirt; I had made a good band about the waist.’
sentences.s103-s27: When the ringing sound of the bell came from behind the elephant, the war-drums began to roar for several miles.
sentences.s103-s28: Someone brought news to the king that Rostam had cut the heart of the army.
sentences.s103-s5: Today I will seize the strap of his waist, drag him and bring him to you.
sentences.s103-s6: Zāl said to him: 'O son, be attentive — today be on your guard.'
sentences.s103-s7: For that Turk in battle is a male dragon; in attack and in malice he is a cloud of calamity.
sentences.s103-s8: His standard is black and his accoutrements are black; his forearms are of iron, his helmet of iron.
